explicit_text: I was born in San Jose, California, on May 14, 1893. My birthplace was on Third Street, just off St. James Park. Years later, when I took my mother to a relative's funeral there, I asked her to show me the exact spot where I was born, which had become the site of the Osen Motor Sales Company garage.

explicit_text: The Osen Motor Sales Company garage was located on the site of my birthplace in San Jose. Interestingly, while I was in Reno, Nevada, I was actually putting a new floor in the Osen Motor Sales garage there. So, this company connected two important places in my life—San Jose and Reno.

explicit_text: I moved to Reno when I was in the eighth grade and attended the Southside School and later the old high school on West and Fifth Street. I graduated from high school there around Christmas of 1910 after three and a half years. Reno was also where I met my future wife, Bunny, who had lived in Pacific Grove when I did but was younger and we never met until Reno.

explicit_text: Shortly after I was born, my family moved to Watsonville, California, where we lived with my mother's parents, the Hamms. My grandfather, Jeremiah Hamm, trained horses for the San Francisco Fire Department and sold them to the department. I remember swimming in the Pajaro River and stealing watermelons and strawberries from the many Chinese residents there, as well as watching boats come in from San Francisco loaded with fruits and vegetables.

explicit_text: We moved to Cripple Creek, Colorado, when I was young, and all I really remember from there was digging little holes and pretending to have my own mine with a headframe. It was a brief and simple memory compared to other places we lived.

explicit_text: After Cripple Creek, we moved to Colorado Springs where my grandfather Edward Ferris lived. He ran a linen and silk business with branches in Colorado Springs and Philadelphia and traveled to Europe annually to buy linens. I have fond memories of playing pinochle with him and taking long walks to places like the Garden of the Gods. I also recall the Antlers Hotel burning down during our time there and the celebration of the century bells at midnight on January 1, 1900.

explicit_text: While living in Colorado Springs, I took long walks with my grandfather Edward Ferris to the Garden of the Gods. I vividly remember being taken by my father to the top of the highest rock there, looking down a narrow ledge that seemed to drop nine miles to the ground. It was an awe-inspiring place that left a lasting impression on me.

explicit_text: During our time in Colorado Springs, the main hotel, the Antlers Hotel, burned down in a spectacular fire. It was a large wooden building, and the fire spread to a nearby lumber yard, making it quite a spectacle to witness. This event stuck in my memory as a dramatic moment of that period.

explicit_text: We moved to Trinidad, Colorado, a coal mining town, where I witnessed some unique local customs like the Penitentes' annual festival involving self-whipping with thorny twigs. I also remember sledding down a steep street to the railroad station and the mischief kids got into by freezing water on sidewalks to skate, which caused many injuries and was eventually stopped. Trinidad was a place of vivid childhood memories and adventures.

explicit_text: In Trinidad, Colorado, I saw the Penitentes, a group of Indians who held a private yearly festival where they whipped themselves on their bare backs with thorny twigs. We kids hid in the sagebrush to watch, careful not to get too close. It was a strange and memorable cultural event from my childhood.

explicit_text: We moved to Pacific Grove, California, where my father worked as a sign painter and played flute in the local orchestra. I learned to fish there and earned money cleaning fishermen's boats. I also played games like hare and hounds, often ending up in Carmel-by-the-Sea, and I remember the deserted Carmel Mission and shipwrecks along the coast. I was in Pacific Grove until about 1906, when we moved after the San Francisco earthquake.

explicit_text: Dr. Ed Dopey Chase was the principal of the high school I attended in Reno. After a physics class prank involving electrical wires went wrong, I ended up having to study in his office and run messages for teachers. He was a memorable figure in my school life, especially because I got blamed for the incident that happened right over his office.
